The company’s improving free cash flow generation provides it with greater flexibility to further develop its AI models to improve credit assessment and explore expansion opportunities into new verticals. At the same time, the significant expansion in FCF margin indicates that Pagaya’s business model is maturing and proving its viability, which could reflect on its valuation as its business could demand a higher valuation multiple.

Moving on to its balance sheet, Pagaya exited Q1 with $229.6 million in cash and held $760 million of investments in loans and securities. The company also has a total debt balance of $672.2 million, of which $536.4 million are long-term debt.

Looking forward, Pagaya is guiding Q2 network volume between $2.3 to $2.5 billion, total revenue between $290 to $310 million, adjusted EBITDA between $75 to $90 million, and GAAP income between breakeven to $10 million. For the full year, the company expects network volume between $9.5 to $11 billion, a reduction from its original guidance issued in Q4 2024. However, the company raised its revenue guidance to a range between $1.175 to $1.3 billion, adjusted EBITDA between $290 to $330 million, and GAAP income between $10 to $45 million.

Better Loan Performance Will Drive Lower Risk Retention

As mentioned earlier, Pagaya exited Q1 with $760 million of investments in loans and securities. The majority of these loans are 2025 and 2024 vintages, representing 70% of the total loans on its balance sheet, while 2023 and 2022, prior vintages represent 19% and 11% of its total loans, respectively.

The significance of this is that the newer loan vintages are performing much better than older vintages, as witnessed by the substantial $235 million impairment charge on poor-performing loans spanning 2021 through 2023. This can be seen by the stable delinquency rates for Pagaya’s personal loan portfolio and declining delinquencies for its auto loan portfolio.

These results are the main driver behind Pagaya’s improving risk retention rates. In Q1, the company purchased loans worth $81.9 million, compared to $261.6 million in the prior year and $155.2 million in the prior quarter. This means that the company retained only 3.41% of its network volume during the quarter, compared to 10.82% and 5.96% in the prior year and quarter, respectively.

The improving performance of Pagaya’s loans is being reflected in its ability to increasingly secure future funding. This is evident by the forward flow agreement with Blue Owl for $2.4 billion over 24 months and the $800 million it raised through ABS in April for its personal loan and auto loan verticals.

Moreover, the company recently received a rating upgrade to its personal loan ABS issued prior to September 2024 by KBRA. This rating upgrade was mainly due to KBRA’s analysis indicating that these notes are exhibiting credit improvements as they have received timely interest payments.

This rating upgrade is a significant validation of the strength and accuracy of Pagaya’s AI underwriting, even in potentially challenging economic environments. In my opinion, this could further improve investor confidence in the company’s loans which could be reflected in lower funding costs, attract new capital to Pagaya’s network, and potentially help it secure additional forward flow agreements in the near future. Accordingly, I expect Pagaya’s risk retention rate to continue declining to around 3% by the end of 2025.

POS Inflection

In addition to potentially helping it secure additional forward flow agreements, the improving performance of Pagaya’s loans is a tailwind to its expansion in the POS vertical, in my opinion. The company recently announced its inaugural AAA-rated $300 million ABS program that is focused on POS financing. This program has a revolving nature over 18 months, allowing the company to reinvest proceeds as interest payments are made, which provides Pagaya with the capacity to fund more than $1 billion of POS loans.

In my opinion, the launch of this ABS program is a major inflection point for Pagaya’s POS vertical, which is already its fastest-growing vertical, per management in the Q1 earnings call. As is, this ABS program allows the company to further penetrate the POS market by enabling its POS lending partners, namely Klarna, to approve a larger number of customers at the point of sale. This is especially significant since it could help Pagaya’s take rate expand over time if these loans are performing well.

With that in mind, the new POS ABS program is targeting borrowers with FICO scores higher than 600, which carries some credit risk. Therefore, it is critical that these loans perform well for Pagaya to expand its take rate and improve its bottom-line by reducing its funding costs. As is, Finsight states that Pagaya’s bonds will yield 1.75% more than benchmark treasury bonds, whereas Affirm (AFRM), which targets borrowers with higher credit scores, had its latest bond yield 0.95% higher than treasuries.

Valuation

At the midpoint of its full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance and its current share price of $15.72, Pagaya is trading at a 5.27 EV/adjusted EBITDA multiple. This indicates that Pagaya remains extremely undervalued relative to the sector median of 14, despite its improving profitability. By applying the sector median EV/EBITDA, my price target for Pagaya is $51.50 per share, representing a 228% upside from its current valuation.

Risks

Although I remain bullish on Pagaya, there are some risks to consider. The main risk to my thesis is the company’s sensitivity to economic downturns. While Pagaya’s AI models are showing significant improvements in underwriting, higher-than-expected job losses or consumer deleveraging efforts could lead to a spike in delinquencies across all loan portfolios, including newer vintages.

Another risk to consider is Pagaya’s reliance on the willingness of institutional investors to buy its ABS or enter forward flow agreements. If a significant shift in investor sentiment away from consumer credit occurs, Pagaya’s funding channels could dry up and potentially see it fund a higher number of loans from its balance sheet, increasing its exposure to credit risk.

Moreover, the company’s latest POS ABS could present a challenge to credit risk due to its focus on riskier consumers. Although Pagaya’s AI is designed to minimize risk, any significant underperformance in these loans could put pressure on the program’s perceived quality. This could affect future ABS pricing, which would be a headwind to profitability.

Conclusion

In summary, I remain bullish on Pagaya and maintain my belief that its shares are extremely undervalued. The company has turned a corner in Q1 2025 with its first GAAP profitable quarter as a public company thanks to its robust topline growth, expanding margin, and efficiently managing costs. This improved financial performance is aided by the improvements in its loans, which are driving increased trust from its funding partners through forward flow agreements.

In my opinion, Pagaya is likely to secure additional forward flow agreements in the near term thanks to its loans’ improving performance, which was validated by a recent rating upgrade by KBRA. Moreover, Pagaya’s expansion in the rapidly growing POS vertical with the launch of its inaugural POS ABS program could further help its future growth by potentially expanding its take rate over time, if these loans replicate the performance of its personal and auto loans. It is for these reasons that I’m upgrading Pagaya to a strong buy with a price target of $51, implying a 228% upside from its current valuation.

data.title: PENN Entertainment: ESPN Bet Weakness Instigates A Proxy Fight
data.description:
PENN Entertainment, Inc. (PENN) is amidst a heating proxy fight. The gambling company’s ESPN Bet online sportsbook has continued to weigh on earnings with no notable market share gains, and activist investor HG Vora has raised the issue as a board election is nearing. PENN’s land-based casinos have continued to report quite stable earnings despite weather disruption in Q1.

I initiated PENN at a Hold rating in my initial November 2024 article on the stock, “PENN Entertainment: ESPN Bet Weakness Strains Land-Based Earnings”. Continued concerns have since pressured the stock price by -31%, significantly underperforming S&P 500’s decline of -3%.

ESPN Bet Remains Weak; A Proxy Fight Ensues

Continuing to weigh on PENN, the company’s ESPN Bet online gambling platform has continued its weak performance in customer acquisition. DraftKings (DKNG) and FanDuel (FLUT) remain the online gaming industry’s two giants, and ESPN Bet has still had a hard time breaking in. According to CasinoReports’ data, ESPN Bet still only has a 3.2% market share in sports betting as of March. The company has an even lower 2.4% market share in iGaming.

The online segment has continued to weigh on earnings. In Q1, excluding gaming taxes, PENN’s Interactive segment generated revenues of $162 million with a massive adjusted EBITDA loss of -$89.0 million. The Interactive segment’s year-on-year revenue growth of 78% did significantly outperform DraftKings’ 20% growth and FanDuel-owner Flutter’s 18% growth in the U.S. that I wrote about in an article, but the overperformance is caused by a still very weak comparison period after ESPN Bet’s initial launch. Growth from more recent quarters remains very subdued, as ESPN Bet’s weak market share performance outlines. The segment’s forward growth outlook remains below DraftKings’ and FanDuel’s based on market share trends, and deep earnings losses make the segment performance concerning.

Both DraftKings and Flutter's U.S. operations have started to report positive EBITDA through operating leverage, but PENN’s significant brand investments and still low market share cause PENN’s online segment to report continued losses. Although the Interactive segment’s losses have moderated, I believe that PENN needs to significantly increase its online market share to reach profitability. The significant $1.5 billion in total payments for a 10-year term to use the ESPN brand requires much more in revenues.

The continued underperformance has caused activist investor HG Vora to be very outspoken on PENN’s management. The activist investor holds a 4.8% stake in PENN. HG Vora added two names to the board of directors earlier this year, agreed to by PENN to avoid a proxy fight. Yet, a massive proxy fight is heating up regardless – HG Vora is trying to appoint all three of its nominees to PENN's board against PENN’s agreement to only appoint two in the June 17th election. PENN has reduced the board’s seats up for election ahead of the vote from three to two, and HG Vora filed a lawsuit against the company due to the decision.

HG Vora and PENN’s management still have very opposing strategic views ahead of the election. While PENN’s management reaffirmed the management’s confidence in PENN’s omnichannel growth strategy in a recent shareholder letter, HG Vora’s recent presentation urges PENN to reconsider the ambitious online sports betting strategy, and highlights PENN's management’s increased compensation in spite of the underperformance. HG Vora’s presentation calls PENN’s sportsbook strategy a risky bet and claims that PENN’s board has failed its core responsibilities, casting doubt on PENN's operations without HG Vora's nominees being appointed.

Ultimately, the upcoming June election will show how much power HG Vora gains over PENN’s strategy. The proxy fight is a significant catalyst to watch, and could sway PENN away from the company’s high sportsbook ambition that has so far burned a significant amount of capital.

PENN’s Land-Based Performance Remains Stable

PENN’s land-based earnings remained relatively stable in Q1. While severe and broad-based weather disruption caused weak traffic in January and February, the segment showed $1.38 billion in revenues, down by just -1.5% year-on-year. Adjusted EBITDAR reached $457 million for the land-based segment, down by –4.6% through reverse operating leverage. The adjusted EBITDAR margin still stood strong at 33.1%, underlining PENN’s good assets in land-based casinos.

According to PENN, land-based gaming volumes stabilized in March and have remained consistent afterwards into early May; PENN’s land-based operations remain stable past weather disruption. The developments in Hollywood Joliet, Hollywood Columbus Hotel Tower, Hollywood Aurora, and M Resort Hotel Tower are nearing completion, adding to a relatively good 2026-2027 growth outlook in the core segment.

PENN’s Valuation Is Fair, But Very Volatile

I updated my discounted cash flow [DCF] model for PENN. The DCF model is still based on moderate growth from online gaming, and quite a good land-based gaming performance.

I have still slightly lowered DCF model expectations; as ESPN Bet continues to lag, I now estimate a slightly lower 2024-2034 revenue CAGR of 3.6%. I lowered the perpetual growth expectation to 2% as well. Based on lower revenue expectations, I also now estimate the EBIT margin to only reach 11%; ESPN Bet still looks to weigh on long-term profitability despite the Interactive segment likely cutting a good share of its massive losses over the mid-term.

With significant CapEx planned for 2025, I lowered my free cash flow expectation for the year. Afterwards, I slightly raised my cash flow conversion estimates for PENN. Lease-related interest is again subtracted from the otherwise unlevered free cash flow.

The estimates put PENN’s fair value estimate at $16.53, 13% above the stock price at the time of writing; a good amount of long-term weakness is already priced in, as my DCF model expects ESPN Bet to continue weighing on solid land-based earnings. At the same time, PENN’s valuation is highly volatile due to an uncertain Interactive segment outlook and the company’s high debt, and the estimated upside merely presents a tiny margin of safety.

High swings in valuation are probably ahead for PENN, as the earnings outlook remains uncertain regarding the Interactive segment - an accurate fair value is very challenging to estimate.

The fair value estimate is down from $22.05 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 10.44%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I have kept the interest rate estimate at 6.73% and the long-term debt-to-equity ratio estimate at 60%. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.53% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.72. With an ESG add-on of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 13.68% and the WACC at 10.44%.

Takeaway

PENN’s online operations remain weak, failing to capture market share and continuing to report significant EBITDA losses. The underperformance has prompted HG Vora to start a proxy fight, which is heating up significantly ahead of the June board election. PENN’s land-based operations have remained stable. While I estimate PENN’s stock to have a fair valuation, I believe that the valuation is likely to see significant volatility ahead. I remain with a Hold rating for PENN.
